lesson.title: ARIMA & Differencing
lesson.body: ARIMA(p, d, q) extends ARMA to non-stationary series by applying d rounds of differencing: ∇x_t = x_t − x_{t-1}. First differencing removes linear trends; second differencing removes quadratic. The unit root test (ADF test) formally tests whether differencing is needed. Seasonal ARIMA SARIMA(p,d,q)(P,D,Q)_s adds seasonal differencing and seasonal AR/MA terms.

rooms.question: What does the 'd' in ARIMA(p, d, q) represent?
answers: The number of times the series is differenced
rooms.explanation: d is the integration order — number of differences needed to achieve stationarity. d=1 handles linear trends, d=2 handles quadratic.

rooms.question: The first difference ∇x_t is defined as:
rooms.explanation: First differencing removes the trend. If x_t has a linear trend, ∇x_t is stationary.

rooms.question: The Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) test has null hypothesis:
answers: The series has a unit root (is non-stationary)
rooms.explanation: ADF H₀: unit root exists (non-stationary). If we reject H₀ (p-value < α), the series is likely stationary and doesn't need differencing.

rooms.question: Order the Box-Jenkins methodology for ARIMA modeling:
items: Check stationarity (ADF test), difference if needed
Identify p, q from ACF/PACF of differenced series
Diagnostic check: residuals should be white noise
rooms.explanation: Box-Jenkins: make stationary → identify orders → estimate → diagnose. Iterate if residuals are not white noise.

rooms.question: A SARIMA(1,1,1)(1,1,1)_12 model is appropriate for:
answers: Monthly data with both trend and annual seasonality
rooms.explanation: SARIMA with d=1 and D=1 handles both trend (regular differencing) and yearly seasonality (seasonal differencing at period 12 for monthly data).

boss.name: The ARIMA Daemon
boss.taunt: You cannot model me without first making me stationary. Difference me. I dare you.

questions.question: After fitting ARIMA, the residuals should resemble:
questions.explanation: Good ARIMA residuals behave like white noise — no remaining autocorrelation. The Ljung-Box test formally checks this.

questions.question: An ARIMA(0,1,0) model is equivalent to:
answers: A random walk: x_t = x_{t-1} + ε_t
questions.explanation: ARIMA(0,1,0): no AR, 1 difference, no MA. That means ∇x_t = ε_t, i.e. x_t = x_{t-1} + ε_t — the random walk.

questions.question: The information criteria AIC penalizes model complexity to avoid overfitting. Prefer the model with:
questions.explanation: AIC = −2logL + 2k. Lower is better — it trades off fit (log-likelihood) against complexity (number of parameters k).
